headline: Mid-summer pause holds: no new enactments, but three federal tax credits in Ways & Means and the Florida ballot measure define the next inflection points.
paragraphs: No bill status changes appeared in today's data refresh — the mid-summer legislative recess has the docket static across all tracked jurisdictions. The governing landscape from recent enactments remains in place: the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act (P.L. 119-101, signed July 11) is the most consequential federal housing law in years, with HUD rulemaking on expanded FHA multifamily loan limits and HOME eligibility now the critical near-term milestone. Pennsylvania HB 96 (Act 27 of 2026, signed July 12) is operational, giving property owners a designated-agent mechanism to receive delinquent-tax notices — portfolio managers should incorporate the designation filing into standard intake. Florida's constitutional property-tax overhaul (HJR 1-F, signed June 16) is set for the November ballot; lenders underwriting Florida income properties should run scenarios on the assessment-cap and revenue-limitation provisions now, before voter approval locks in the framework.

The most consequential pending federal items are three tax bills parked in House Ways and Means. The LIHTC reform bill (H.R. 2725) would expand annual credit allocations and strengthen the 9% credit — the single biggest lever in the affordable housing finance stack. Alongside it, the Workforce Housing Tax Credit Act (H.R. 8626) would open a new tax-equity lane for middle-income projects that currently don't qualify for LIHTC but can't pencil without subsidy in most markets. The Revitalizing Downtowns and Main Streets Act (H.R. 2410) would create a federal investment credit for converting non-residential buildings to affordable housing, directly subsidizing the office-to-residential conversion wave without the historic-tax-credit requirement for historic buildings. Any movement on this trio in the fall session would reshape deal structures across the development pipeline; the committee's schedule after recess is the key variable to monitor.

At the state level, three items merit close attention. In Pennsylvania, the anti-predatory listing-contract bill (H.B. 986) passed the House and sits in Senate Urban Affairs — if enacted, it mirrors the New York law signed in December 2025 and would require commission-disclosure restructuring statewide. Pennsylvania's home-inspector licensing overhaul is taking two bites at the same apple: SB 907 was tabled on June 25, and the House version (HB 2645) was re-committed to Rules on June 24 — the industry is watching which vehicle survives into fall committee work. In New York, the Deed Protection Act (A09554) passed the Assembly and is in Senate Judiciary; its passage would bar lenders from foreclosing on mortgages they know or should know rest on fraudulently obtained deeds, with direct implications for title diligence and underwriting standards on any property with a chain-of-title question.
